subject: Re: import better motion correction parameters?
body:
Hi Chuck,

yes, that would work to some extent. You can replace the global shifts with 
those from MC2 (please note the values in Warp are in Angstrom, and specify 
the vector the frame needs to be shifted by to be in alignment with all the 
other frames). However, it might be tricky to get local motion out of MC2 
and to translate it into Warp's format. RELION's implementation writes out 
the polynomial factors, but they still need to be translated into shifts.

Once you've updated the values, reload the files in Warp, deactivate the 
"Motion" section in the processing settings, change e.g. the pixel size 
anisotropy angle (assuming you're not using anisotropic pixels, it will 
just force Warp to reprocess everything), and restart processing.

I'd be curious to take a look at the processing settings and a few of the 
failing movies if you can share them confidentially. Maybe a quick 
adjustment to the settings or minor changes in the algorithm can solve the 
problem.

Cheers,
Dimitry

On Sunday, October 25, 2020 at 11:36:32 PM UTC+1 charles....@yale.edu wrote:

> Hi Dmitry, I'm trying to use WARP with a data set where the beam shift a 
> bit unstable, and "jumps" by large amounts in a seemingly random fashion. 
> We got MotionCorr2 to give us decent alignments, but within WARP there are 
> frequent failures (i.e. diagnostic images that are "ghosted" or blurry). It 
> is a valuable enough data set (time and $$$$ to re-collect) that it would 
> be worth a bit of time to get this working. 
>
> Looking in the warp-generated XML files, I can see where the shift 
> parameters are stored. It would be easy for me to write a script to "hack" 
> WARP by inserting our MotionCorr2 shifts into the XML files after WARP 
> generates its own (mis)alignments. Will that be enough to fool WARP, or is 
> that just going to make it unhappy?
>
> best, Chuck
>
